title: What happens when your AI employee gets something wrong
excerpt: AI employees aren't perfect. They make mistakes, misread context, and occasionally do something dumb. Here's how we handle it and what to expect.
author: Simon Insinger
content:
## They will mess up

I want to be upfront about this because most AI companies won't: your AI employee will make mistakes.

It will misread an email tone. It will schedule something at a weird time. It will send a follow-up that doesn't quite land. This is going to happen, especially in the first few weeks.

The question isn't whether it makes mistakes. It's whether the mistakes are acceptable relative to what it gets right — and whether it gets better over time.

## The types of mistakes

From what we've seen, AI employee errors fall into a few buckets.

Misread context. The AI didn't understand that "let's circle back next week" meant "I'm not interested." It followed up anyway. This is the most common early mistake and it gets better as we tune the system to your communication style.

Missing information. The AI booked a meeting but didn't know about an internal conflict that wasn't on the calendar. This happens when the AI doesn't have access to everything it needs. Usually fixable by connecting more data sources.

Wrong prioritization. It flagged the wrong email as urgent or scheduled a low-priority meeting over a high-priority block. This improves as the AI learns your preferences, but it takes a few weeks of feedback.

Hallucinated details. Rarely, but it happens — the AI includes a fact in a briefing that isn't quite right. We build in source-checking and confidence thresholds to minimize this, but we can't eliminate it entirely.

## How we handle it

Every AI employee we build launches with guardrails:

- Approval workflows for sensitive actions (sending emails, booking with external contacts)
- Confidence thresholds — if the AI isn't sure, it asks instead of guessing
- Human escalation paths for anything ambiguous
- Activity logs so you can review what it did and correct course

During the first 2 weeks after launch, we monitor closely and tune based on real mistakes. Most clients see error rates drop significantly by week 3.

## The honest comparison

Here's the thing people forget: human employees make mistakes too. They forget follow-ups, double-book meetings, send emails with typos, and have off days.

The difference is that when an AI employee makes a mistake, you can fix the underlying pattern. When it learns not to do something, it never does it again. A human might make the same mistake next month because they were tired or distracted.

That said, AI mistakes can feel different. There's something jarring about a machine getting something wrong that a human would obviously get right. The first time it happens, you'll question the whole setup. That's normal.

## What we tell new clients

Expect a bumpy first two weeks. You'll catch mistakes. You'll wonder if this was the right call. Then somewhere around week 3, you'll realize it's handling things you forgot you delegated, and the mistakes are getting rare.

That's the pattern we see with almost every deployment. The early friction is real, but it passes.
